summary: The paper presents a well-structured empirical null result with the Phi Modulator Framework as organizational infrastructure and the Phi Invariance observation as the core finding. Key strengths: rigorous statistical methodology (paired t-tests, Bonferroni, TOST, power analysis), clean experimental design with 105 runs, and the CWD/random-mask insight. Critical weaknesses: (1) Figure 8 includes PMP-WD data points that are absent from the paper's method set and tables -- a stale figure from a previous iteration; (2) title overclaims causation ('Why') when the paper only shows correlation; (3) CSI metric has zero predictive value but occupies significant space; (4) ImageNet experiments still missing after 5+ iterations; (5) statistical power too low (N=3) for the equivalence claims made; (6) massive proposal-paper scope gap (4 theorems promised, 0 delivered); (7) half_lambda BEM value in Figure 4 appears at ~0.0 but Table 6 reports BEM=0.500 -- a figure-table contradiction persisting from iter_007.

findings.description: Figure 8 (theorem2_validation.png) includes PMP-WD data points (pink/red dots in legend) that do not appear in the paper's method set (Section 4.1 lists 7 methods; PMP-WD is not among them). The paper never describes PMP-WD experiments, never reports PMP-WD accuracy, and the method appears in no table. This is a stale figure carried forward from a previous iteration when PMP-WD was part of the design. N=18 method-seed combinations implies 6 methods x 3 seeds, but the paper has 7 methods -- the count is inconsistent either way. This is a desk-reject-level error: a figure showing data for an undescribed method.
findings.suggestion: Regenerate Figure 8 using only the 7 methods described in Section 4.1. If PMP-WD data was used in the correlation computation, the rho and p-values are also wrong and must be recomputed. Rename theorem2_validation.png to alignment_analysis.png since there is no Theorem 2 in the paper.

findings.description: Figure 4 (BEM scatter plot): half_lambda appears clustered near BEM~0.0 on CIFAR-10, but Table 6 reports half_lambda BEM=0.500. This figure-table contradiction has been flagged in iter_007, iter_008, AND iter_009 -- four consecutive iterations with the same bug. Either the figure reads stale data or the BEM computation in the plotting script is incorrect. A paper with contradictory quantitative information between its figures and tables is not submittable.
findings.suggestion: Debug regenerate_figures.py to verify BEM values match Table 6. Regenerate fig3_bem_vs_accuracy.png with correct data. Visually verify the regenerated figure before proceeding.

findings.description: ImageNet experiments are completely absent. The project constraints mandate ImageNet. The proposal planned Phase 4 (ResNet-50/ImageNet). The methodology includes it. Five iterations have passed with zero ImageNet results. The paper's own limitations acknowledge the gap. For a paper claiming to characterize 'when dynamic WD matters,' restricting to CIFAR-10/100 (32x32 images, <300K params for ResNet-20) severely limits credibility at top venues.
findings.suggestion: Before any text editing, diagnose ImageNet failure: SSH into server, verify data path, run a 1-epoch smoke test. Then run at minimum constant + cwd_hard + no_wd x 3 seeds = 9 runs on ResNet-50/ImageNet. If Phi Invariance breaks on ImageNet, that is an equally valuable finding.

findings.description: Massive scope collapse between proposal and paper. Proposal promises 6 contributions including Lyapunov Variance Decomposition (Theorem 2), Budget Equivalence Theorem (Theorem 3), PMP Optimal Control Derivation (Theorem 4), Controller Taxonomy, and AIS threshold characterization. The delivered paper has ZERO theorems -- it is a purely empirical taxonomy plus null result. The proposal still references Lyapunov stability theory and Pontryagin's Maximum Principle in its title and abstract. Any reviewer comparing proposal artifacts against the paper will see a fundamentally different scope.
findings.suggestion: Update proposal.md to match the empirical-only scope of the current paper. Remove all references to Lyapunov, PMP, optimal control, Theorems 1-4, and controller taxonomy. The paper is stronger as a clean empirical contribution than as a failed theoretical one.

findings.description: The title 'The Phi Invariance Conjecture: Why Dynamic Weight Decay Methods Are Equivalent Under AdamW' makes a causal claim ('Why') that the paper does not support. The paper shows THAT methods produce equivalent accuracy, not WHY. The mechanistic hypothesis (AdamW's adaptive scaling subsumes modulation) is plausible speculation supported by correlational evidence (weight norm convergence), not a causal proof. There is no ablation of AdamW's second-moment estimation, no theoretical derivation of why adaptive scaling should subsume Phi modulation, and no causal intervention experiment.
findings.suggestion: Change 'Why' to 'When' or remove it entirely. E.g.: 'The Phi Invariance Conjecture: Dynamic Weight Decay Methods Are Equivalent Under AdamW on CIFAR-Scale Benchmarks.'

findings.description: CSI metric has arbitrary weights (w1=0.4, w2=0.3, w3=0.3), combines dimensionally incompatible quantities (CV of weight norms, log condition number, CV of effective LR), and the paper admits CSI does not predict accuracy (rho < 0.3, p > 0.3). The sensitivity analysis tests only three nearly-identical weight sets. CSI occupies a full definition (Section 3.4), a table column (Table 6), a heatmap column (Figure 5), and discussion text -- all for zero demonstrated predictive or diagnostic value. This was flagged as a lesson-learned ('CSI is not a contribution') and remains unaddressed.
findings.suggestion: Demote CSI to supplementary material or an 'exploratory diagnostic' subsection. Remove it from Section 1.3 contributions list. Redistribute the freed space to BEM and AIS analysis, which do provide interpretable results.

findings.description: The 'Phi Invariance Conjecture' is overstated for the evidence base. The paper tests 2 small datasets (CIFAR-10/100, 32x32), 2 architectures with BN (ResNet-20 ~270K params, VGG-16-BN ~15M params), and 7 methods. Calling this a 'conjecture' implies a formal mathematical claim with generality. What the paper shows is an empirical regularity on CIFAR-scale benchmarks. The conjecture extends to 'sufficiently overparameterized problems with batch normalization' but has no evidence outside CIFAR. Without ImageNet validation, the conjecture's scope is CIFAR-specific.
findings.suggestion: Reframe as an 'empirical regularity' or 'observed phenomenon' until ImageNet validation confirms it holds at scale. Alternatively, provide explicit falsification criteria with concrete experimental conditions that would disprove the conjecture.

findings.description: Statistical power is insufficient for the claims. N=3 seeds gives 80% power to detect effects >= 0.7% (as stated in Section 5.1). The paper claims 'no method produces statistically distinguishable accuracy' but this is a power limitation, not a positive finding. Absence of evidence != evidence of absence. TOST confirms equivalence at +/-1.0% for only 6 of 12 comparisons. The abstract and conclusion state equivalence without the power caveat.
findings.suggestion: Add 2 seeds (N=5) for the 4 most important comparisons (constant vs cwd_hard, swd, cosine_schedule, no_wd on CIFAR-10). Cost: 8 runs, ~2 GPU-hours. The abstract must state 'equivalent within our detection threshold of 0.7%' until power is improved.

findings.description: The abstract claims '105 controlled experiments across 7 methods, 2 optimizers, 2 architectures, 2 datasets, and 3 seeds' implying a fully crossed design (7x2x2x2x3 = 168 runs). The actual design is partial: ResNet-20 is fully crossed (84 runs) but VGG-16-BN is only SGD/CIFAR-10 (21 runs). The abstract's listing of factors creates a misleading impression of completeness.
findings.suggestion: Revise abstract: '105 experiments including a full crossed design for ResNet-20 (7 methods x 2 optimizers x 2 datasets x 3 seeds = 84 runs) and a targeted VGG-16-BN validation (21 runs).'

findings.description: Section 5.7 draws a directional conclusion from two non-significant results: 'the directional difference (|Delta rho| = 0.424) suggests cumulative alignment may be a more informative measure than worst-case alignment.' Comparing two non-significant correlations without a formal test (Steiger's z-test for dependent correlations) is statistically improper. This was flagged in lessons_learned and remains unfixed.
findings.suggestion: Replace with: 'Neither cumulative nor worst-case alignment deviation predicts generalization gap in our sample (both p > 0.1). Larger-scale validation is needed.' Remove the directional claim entirely or add a formal Steiger's z-test.

findings.description: VGG-16-BN is only tested with SGD on CIFAR-10. The paper interprets VGG-16-BN results as evidence that 'BN enables Phi Invariance even without adaptive optimization,' but this conclusion requires a 2x2 design: (BN/no-BN) x (AdamW/SGD). Without VGG-16-BN + AdamW and VGG-16 without BN + SGD, the attribution to BN vs architecture vs optimizer is confounded.
findings.suggestion: Add VGG-16-BN + AdamW on CIFAR-10 (7 methods x 3 seeds = 21 runs). If VGG-16-BN + AdamW also shows invariance, it confirms the BN hypothesis. If not, the current interpretation is wrong.

findings.description: Section 6.2 reports preliminary non-BN results: 'ResNet-20 without BN (SGD, CIFAR-10): constant=87.74, CWD=87.63, spread=0.11pp.' But only 2 of 7 methods were tested. These results, if valid, actually contradict the paper's hypothesis -- the spread is NARROWER without BN (0.11pp) than with BN under AdamW (0.25pp), opposite to what the BN-as-mechanism hypothesis predicts. The paper hand-waves this with 'limiting generalizability' but does not address the contradiction.
findings.suggestion: Either expand non-BN experiments to all 7 methods to test the hypothesis properly, or remove the preliminary results that contradict the main narrative. Do not present 2/7 methods as evidence for a 7-method claim.

findings.description: The Phi framework's novelty is organizational, not mathematical. Defining phi(t, theta, g) as a per-parameter modulation function and showing existing methods are special cases is a standard taxonomy exercise. The framework does not derive new properties, predict new phenomena, or enable new methods. D'Angelo et al. (2024) already showed 'WD is never useful as regularization in modern settings,' which partially subsumes the Phi Invariance observation. The paper needs to clearly distinguish its contribution from D'Angelo's finding.
findings.suggestion: Add a paragraph in Related Work explicitly contrasting with D'Angelo: they showed WD doesn't regularize; we show dynamic modulation of WD is additionally futile under AdamW. The Phi framework enables this systematic comparison. Reframe the framework as enabling infrastructure, not a standalone contribution.

findings.description: Figure numbering in the text does not match file naming. Text references Figures 2-8 but filenames are fig1_taxonomy.png through fig6_sgd_vs_adamw_norms.png, plus multi_arch_comparison.png and theorem2_validation.png. Figure 8 references a file called theorem2_validation.png for content that is actually cumulative alignment analysis -- no Theorem 2 exists in the paper.
findings.suggestion: Rename theorem2_validation.png to alignment_analysis.png. Ensure a consistent numbering scheme between text and files.

findings.description: CSI's Hessian condition number component requires power iteration but the paper does not specify: (a) loss Hessian vs weight-space Hessian, (b) number of iterations, (c) convergence criterion, (d) computed on train or test data, (e) mini-batch or full-batch. The UnifiedAdamW/UnifiedSGD optimizer classes are described at API level but no code link is provided. SWD's running mean computation is underspecified.
findings.suggestion: Add supplementary pseudocode for CSI/AIS/BEM. Provide code repository link. Specify GPU type and training wall-clock time.

findings.description: Several cited papers have only arXiv IDs with 2026 dates (Ferbach et al., 2026; Chen et al., 2026b). As of March 2026, some of these may be preprints that have not been peer-reviewed. The paper should distinguish between peer-reviewed publications and preprints.
findings.suggestion: Mark preprints explicitly as 'arXiv preprint' in citations. Verify ICLR 2026 acceptances.

metric_gaming_risks: The paper reports 'best test accuracy over 200 epochs' as the primary metric. With only 3 seeds, the best-epoch selection can amplify seed-specific artifacts. Final-epoch accuracy should be reported alongside for comparison.

TOST equivalence margins of +/-0.5% and +/-1.0% are generous. In communities where 0.1% improvements are reported as significant, a 1.0% equivalence margin is too lenient. The paper should justify why these margins are appropriate.

BEM is computed as an expected value across parameters and time. Two methods with identical BEM=0.5 could have very different temporal/spatial distributions, producing different dynamics. BEM masks distributional heterogeneity.
novelty_risks: The Phi framework is a notational abstraction. The real question is whether it enables new insights -- the Phi Invariance observation could be discovered without the framework by simply running the same experiments.

D'Angelo et al. (2024) showed WD is 'never useful as regularization,' which partially subsumes the null result. The paper needs to articulate what is new beyond D'Angelo's observation: specifically, that even the training dynamics modulation is irrelevant under AdamW.

AIS is Spearman correlation between cosine similarity and loss change -- standard statistical measures applied to standard quantities. Calling it a 'standardized diagnostic metric' overstates novelty.
reproducibility_gaps: CSI Hessian condition number: loss vs weight-space Hessian unspecified; power iteration count unspecified; train vs test data unspecified; mini-batch vs full-batch unspecified.
UnifiedAdamW/UnifiedSGD optimizer implementation not provided. Reimplementing the Phi modulator interface with all 7 methods requires non-trivial engineering.
GPU type, training wall-clock time, and mixed precision usage are not reported.
SWD's gradient-norm running mean computation (EMA decay, batch mean, global mean) is not detailed.
